did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically seven convolutional neural network architectures (DenseNet121, EfficientNetB1, EfficientNetB5, EfficientNetB7, InceptionV3, ResNet50, Xception, and a custom model) with transfer learning from ImageNet, to classify dermoscopic skin lesion images from the HAM10000 dataset. The study evaluated their performance in skin cancer detection and classification tasks.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a performance analysis of seven convolutional neural network architectures (DenseNet121, EfficientNet variants, InceptionV3, ResNet50, Xception, and a custom model) for skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology (transfer learning, preprocessing), quantitative results (accuracy, AUC, loss metrics), and error analysis, which are characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: Performance Analysis of Convolutional Architectures for Image Pattern Recognition
journal: 2025 IEEE/IEIE International Conference on Consumer Electronics-Asia (ICCE-Asia)
brief_summary: This study evaluated the performance of seven convolutional neural network architectures for classifying dermoscopic skin lesion images from the HAM10000 dataset. The models, including DenseNet121, EfficientNet variants, InceptionV3, ResNet50, Xception, and a custom model, utilized transfer learning with ImageNet weights and preprocessing techniques like hair removal. DenseNet121 achieved the highest validation accuracy of 86.0% and an AUC of 0.963, outperforming other architectures such as EfficientNetB1 and InceptionV3. The custom model demonstrated significantly lower performance with only 40.8% accuracy. Error analysis revealed classification difficulties between actinic keratosis and basal cell carcinoma classes. The results provide a quantitative comparison of architectural performance for dermatoscopic classification applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. The paper notes it is an aggressive form of skin cancer and discusses classification difficulties and AUC metrics for this class across various models.

One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. It is described as a common benign condition and was frequently correctly classified by the models (e.g., EfficientNetB1 correctly classified 146 cases).
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. The paper highlights classification difficulties between actinic keratosis and basal cell carcinoma classes.

One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. The paper notes perfect classification (AUC 1.00) for AKIEC (Actinic Keratosis and Intraepithelial Carcinoma) in some models but mentions misclassifications involving actinic keratosis.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. The paper uses 'BKL' in error matrix analysis and ROC curve discussions, noting AUC deviations for this class in certain models.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. The paper reports perfect classification (AUC 1.00) for DF in several models.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. The paper reports perfect classification (AUC 1.00) for VASC in several models.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Neoplasm)
all_task_types.details: The paper evaluates seven convolutional neural network architectures for classifying dermoscopic skin lesion images from the HAM10000 dataset. The task is explicitly defined as 'skin lesion classification' and 'classifying dermatoscopic images'. The models predict discrete classes (e.g., NV, MEL, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC) using transfer learning. Performance is measured using accuracy, AUC (Area Under the ROC Curve), and loss, which are standard metrics for classification tasks. The study analyzes error matrices and ROC curves for class prediction.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large dataset of dermoscopic skin lesion images used for the study. The paper references it as the source of the images analyzed, noting it is a large dataset of skin lesion images.

use.training: Used for training the convolutional neural network architectures after preprocessing (intensity normalization, morphological operations for hair removal).
use.validation: Used for validation to evaluate model performance (accuracy, AUC, loss).
use.testing: Not explicitly reported as a separate test set; validation metrics are the primary performance indicators reported.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

HAM10000.notes: DenseNet121 achieved the highest validation accuracy (86.0%) and AUC (0.963). EfficientNetB1 demonstrated strong performance with an AUC of 0.969. The custom model showed significantly lower performance with 40.8% accuracy. Error analysis revealed classification difficulties between actinic keratosis (AKIEC) and basal cell carcinoma (BCC) classes. Models such as EfficientNetB1, EfficientNetB5, DenseNet121, and ResNet50 showed perfect AUC (1.00) for AKIEC, BCC, DF, and VASC classes. Misclassifications were observed primarily in actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, and basal cell carcinoma, whereas melanoma and nevus were classified with fewer errors.

architecture_used.main_model: Seven convolutional neural network architectures: DenseNet121, EfficientNetB1, EfficientNetB5, EfficientNetB7, InceptionV3, ResNet50, Xception, and a custom model.
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning implementation using pre-trained ImageNet weights for all models except the custom model, which was built from scratch.
architecture_used.input_size: 240x240 pixels with three RGB color channels (specifically reported for the custom model; other models likely used standard ImageNet input size of 224x224, but not explicitly stated).

DenseNet121.details: Dense connectivity patterns utilizing feature map concatenation.

EfficientNetB1.details: Mobile Inverted Bottleneck Convolution (MBConv) blocks, Swish activation.

EfficientNetB5.details: Scalable architecture using compound coefficient methodology.

EfficientNetB7.details: Scalable architecture using compound coefficient methodology.

InceptionV3.details: Inception modules with parallel convolutions (1x1, 3x3, 5x5) and pooling layers.

ResNet50.details: Residual blocks with skip connections, Batch Normalization, ReLU activation, Global Average Pooling (GAP), and final fully connected layer with Softmax.

Xception.details: Depthwise separable convolutions replacing standard convolutions.

Custom.details: Built from scratch. Input layer (240x240 RGB), Conv layer (32 3x3 filters, ReLU), MaxPooling (2x2 window).

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (standard TensorFlow/Keras default likely used, but not specified).
training_details.loss_function: Categorical Crossentropy (implied by multi-class classification context and 'Loss' metric reporting), though not explicitly named.
training_details.hardware: Google Colab Pro virtual processors, NVIDIA A100 GPU (40 GB VRAM), 32 GB RAM.
training_details.software: Python 3.8, TensorFlow 2.5.0, Keras 2.4.3, OpenCV 4.5.1, Scikit-learn 0.24.2, NumPy 1.19.5, Pandas 1.2.4, Matplotlib 3.4.2.

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer with Softmax activation
classification_or_output_head.details: Final fully connected layer with Softmax completes the model (specifically noted for ResNet50; implied for others).

preprocessing_techniques: Intensity normalization: All images were normalized to have pixel values within the range [0, 1].

Hair removal: Morphological operations including 'black hat' morphological transformation to extract hair characteristics, binarization, dilation to enhance hair areas, and interpolation to reconstruct obscured pixels using surrounding values.
Artifact/Noise removal: Morphological operations such as erosion and dilatation were used to remove noise and artifacts.
Image resizing: The custom model accepted input images of 240x240 pixels; other models likely used standard ImageNet input size (224x224), though not explicitly detailed for all architectures.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper mentions that 'Data preparation includes image preprocessing and augmentation to increase the diversity of the training set' in the Introduction, but does not specify any particular augmentation techniques (e.g., rotation, flipping, zooming) used during the actual experiments. The study primarily relied on preprocessing (intensity normalization, hair removal via morphological operations) and transfer learning from ImageNet weights.

conclusions: DenseNet121 achieved the highest validation performance among the tested architectures, with 86.0% accuracy and an AUC of 0.963.
EfficientNetB1 demonstrated strong performance with 83.2% accuracy and an AUC of 0.969, comparable to InceptionV3.
The custom model, built from scratch without transfer learning, performed significantly worse than pre-trained models, achieving only 40.8% accuracy and an AUC of 0.7349.
Models such as EfficientNetB1, EfficientNetB5, DenseNet121, and ResNet50 achieved perfect AUC scores (1.00) for the AKIEC, BCC, DF, and VASC classes.

Classification difficulties and misclassifications were primarily observed between actinic keratosis (AKIEC) and basal cell carcinoma (BCC), as well as within benign keratosis (BKL) and melanoma (MEL) classes.
Grad-CAM analysis confirmed that the models focused on lesion regions relevant for discrimination, validating the influence of model architecture and preprocessing on classification outcomes.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding class imbalance and suggests that future work should assess the potential contribution of clinical metadata to improve diagnostic reliability.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known for significant class imbalance and multi-source bias; no external validation on independent datasets was performed.
The custom model, built from scratch without transfer learning, performed significantly worse (40.8% accuracy) than pre-trained models, highlighting the necessity of transfer learning for this task.
Classification difficulties were specifically noted between actinic keratosis (AKIEC) and basal cell carcinoma (BCC), as well as within benign keratosis (BKL) and melanoma (MEL) classes.

Several models (EfficientNetB1, EfficientNetB5, DenseNet121, ResNet50) achieved perfect AUC (1.00) for AKIEC, BCC, DF, and VASC classes, which may indicate overfitting or lack of discriminative difficulty for these specific classes in this dataset.
The paper acknowledges class imbalance as a limitation and suggests future work should incorporate clinical metadata to improve diagnostic reliability.
Data augmentation techniques were mentioned in the introduction but not explicitly specified or detailed in the methodology or results sections.
The custom model input size was 240x240 pixels, while other models likely used standard ImageNet input size (224x224), introducing potential inconsistency in preprocessing across architectures.
No statistical hypothesis testing or confidence intervals were reported for the performance metrics.
The study was funded by the Science Committee of the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Republic of Kazakhstan, which may introduce funding bias.
